Pace, pace, lead, pacing and leading. If this is the strategy by which you essentially enter someone's world where they are, tonally, right? And then you pace them, you pace them, and you lead them in the direction you want them to go. In other words, let's say I wrote my first loop and I say, I like you, what you're saying, Bill? But let me ask you a question. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea, right? And he says, yeah, it sounds pretty good. So what do I do there? He says, yeah, it sounds pretty good. Where does that level of certainty land him? Where does that tonality land him on the certain scale? He goes, yeah, it sounds pretty good. That's like about a six. It's not like, yeah, it sounds really great. That's an eight, right? He says, yeah, it sounds pretty good. She said about a six. So what I wouldn't do is I wouldn't respond by saying exactly, it really is an amazing idea. If you do that, you break rapport, right? He six said, wait a second, you're not listening to me. And I wasn't that enthusiastic. He knows the tonality he gave you. He was telegraphing to you or she was telegraphing to you that, you know, hey, that's my level of certainty. So he said, yeah, it sounds pretty good. So I would never say exactly, Bill, it really is amazing. He's like, oh my God, here we go. Another, you know, fricking hard pressure sales president. So what I do instead is I say exactly, it really is a great idea. It's like, I enter the same place tonally and then I will pace him, meaning I will talk with the same tonality and then I will talk with the same tonality again. And then after I pace him, I'll leave him tonally in the direction I want to go. In other words, I say exactly, it really is a great idea. In fact, let me say this, the true beauty is it also has this feature, this feature and that feature. In other words, I'm in the same tonality. I say, and it also has this feature, that feature and that feature. But however, now watch, now when you take those two things together and all of a sudden now, I start to increase my tone, my certainty. I actually set the ratchet up my level of certainty in my tonality and I slowly increase it, increase it and I start approaching peak tonality about halfway through. Guys, I can't tell you how powerful this is. Listen, it's almost hard for me to do artificially here. So what happens is when someone says, yeah, it sounds pretty good, I say exactly, it really is a great idea. In fact, let me say this, the true beauty is it also has this feature, that feature and that feature. So I stay at that same level of certainty. I say, and then it also has this feature, that feature and that feature. And again, I stay at the same level of certainty, but then I slowly start rationing up my level of certainty. But then it also has this feature, that feature and that feature. And when you combine those four things together, Bill, it gives you this, this and that. And I start getting really excited. And when I end my presentation here, I'm like, you see what I'm saying, Bill? Does the idea make sense to you? And now he's like, oh my God, yeah. Now, and now he'll match my level of certainty in his tone. Guys, this is magic, something like a magic trick. That's how well it works. I'm dead serious. In other words, so what you're doing here is you're entering the prospects world where they are. So they say, yeah, it sounds pretty good. You say exactly, it really is a great idea. But you want to move them to a much higher level of emotional certainty as well as logical certainty. So what you're going to do now is your secondary language pattern is designed to move them higher on the level of logical certainty. And we use pace, pace, leave the tonal shifts to move them to a high level of emotional certainty. In other words, if you give your whole amazing linguistic pitch, you just see all the words are great, but you don't use pace, pace, lead, when you're done, you'll be saying, you see what I'm saying, Bill? Does the idea make sense to you? He'll say, yeah, it makes a lot of sense. He still hasn't been moved emotionally and his tone of voice will still be at that lower level. So in order to move someone in one swift spoof, both logically and emotionally, we apply this strategy of pace, pace, leave. So you say, watch, I say, I hear what you're saying, Bill. Does the idea make sense to you? Do you like the idea? That's my first loop. He says, yeah, it sounds pretty good. And I say, exactly, it really is a great idea. In fact, let me say this, the true booty is it also has, so I'm right where he was. But remember, I want him, when I'm done with this presentation and I say, you see what I'm saying, Bill? I want to say, oh my God, yeah, Jordan, I love you. I need to move him emotionally. I do that with my tonality, but I can't do it all at once. If I just come in like gangbusting, and Bill, exactly, it really isn't, he'll be, whoa, whoa, I break rapport. It also has, like, oh, but it also has some of his more information. And then I introduce slowly, but surely, this intense certainty into my tone. That's the ticket. And by doing that, what happens is, Bill not even knowing it, especially, now this works, remember, your words also have to be awesome. And you've done that because you've spent the time, you've worked on your pattern, right? You've spent the time to work on your language pattern, your secondary pattern, and that pattern is designed to pick up right where your initial presentation left off, and you're creating this scream from the hilltops airtight logical cases, I say, right?
